title: Gustave Brion

infobox.value: (1824–1877) was a French painter and illustrator best known for his depictions of rural life in Alsace and for his illustrations of [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo")’s *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables "Les Misérables")* and *[The Hunchback of Notre-Dame](/wiki/The_Hunchback_of_Notre-Dame "The Hunchback of Notre-Dame")*.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)
* *Les Misérables*, by Victor Hugo (Paris: Pagnerre, 1862), first illustrated edition, with character portraits by Brion[[8]](#cite_note-8)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French painter and illustrator (1824–1877)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Gustave_Brion_-_photographie_Antoine_Meyer.jpg/250px-Gustave_Brion_-_photographie_Antoine_Meyer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gustave_Brion_-_photographie_Antoine_Meyer.jpg)

Gustave Brion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Javert.jpg/250px-Javert.jpg)](/wiki/File:Javert.jpg)

*[Javert](/wiki/Javert "Javert")*, from *Les Misérables* by Victor Hugo, published in 1862.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Un_mariage_protestant.jpg/250px-Un_mariage_protestant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Un_mariage_protestant.jpg)

*A Wedding in Alsace*, 1872

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Gustave_brion_hochzeitsprozession_1873.jpg/250px-Gustave_brion_hochzeitsprozession_1873.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gustave_brion_hochzeitsprozession_1873.jpg)

*[Procession](/wiki/Procession "Procession") in Strasbourg*, 1873

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Brion_Gustave-Les_P%C3%A8lerins_de_Sainte_Odile.jpg/250px-Brion_Gustave-Les_P%C3%A8lerins_de_Sainte_Odile.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brion_Gustave-Les_P%C3%A8lerins_de_Sainte_Odile.jpg)

*The pilgrims of Sainte Odile* ([Unterlinden Museum](/wiki/Unterlinden_Museum "Unterlinden Museum")), Colmar

**Gustave Brion** (1824–1877) was a French painter and illustrator best known for his depictions of rural life in Alsace and for his illustrations of [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo")’s *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables "Les Misérables")* and *[The Hunchback of Notre-Dame](/wiki/The_Hunchback_of_Notre-Dame "The Hunchback of Notre-Dame")*.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

He was born at [Rothau](/wiki/Rothau "Rothau") in the department of [Bas-Rhin](/wiki/Bas-Rhin "Bas-Rhin") on 24 October 1824 and studied in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") under the painter [Gabriel-Cristophe Guérin](/wiki/Gabriel-Christophe_Gu%C3%A9rin "Gabriel-Christophe Guérin") and then the sculptor [Andreas Friedrich](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Hanno "Wilhelm von Hanno"). In 1847, his exhibited *Intérieur à Dambach* at the [Salon of 1847](/wiki/Salon_of_1847 "Salon of 1847").[[2]](#cite_note-2) A few years later, he moved to a studio on rue Notre-Dame-des-Champs, in the same building as Realist artists Jules Breton and François Bonvin.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Brion gained recognition for his genre paintings depicting the peasant life and customs of Alsace, though he occasionally painted historical subjects, such as *The Siege of a Town by Romans under Julius Caesar*, commissioned by Napoleon III. He earned a Second-Class Medal at the Paris [Salon of 1853](/wiki/Salon_of_1853 "Salon of 1853") for his paintings *Schlitteurs de la Forêt-Noire* and P*otato Harvest during an Inundation*. The former was later destroyed during the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) In 1863, his work *Les Fleurs du Pays* earned him a First-Class Medal at the Salon of 1863, along with the [Legion of Honour](/wiki/Legion_of_Honour "Legion of Honour"). Additional honors followed at the Exposition Universelle of 1867 and the Salon of 1868.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

In addition to painting, Brion worked as a book illustrator. He designed over 200 illustrations for the first edition of Hugo's novel *Les Misérables,*[[5]](#cite_note-5) including the first published portrayal of [Inspector Javert](/wiki/Javert "Javert").[[6]](#cite_note-6) His illustrations for the author's *The Hunchback of Notre-Dame* depicted [Quasimodo](/wiki/Quasimodo "Quasimodo") and [Esmeralda](/wiki/Esm%C3%A9ralda_(The_Hunchback_of_Notre-Dame) "Esméralda (The Hunchback of Notre-Dame)").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

Despite his success in Paris, Brion remained deeply attached to his native Alsace, and the annexation of the region by Germany after the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") in 1871 was a personal loss for him. He died on 3 November 1877 at the age of 53.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

Brion was a grandnephew of [Friederike Brion](/wiki/Friederike_Brion "Friederike Brion"), the muse of [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe")’s early poetry.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Principal works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gustave_Brion&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Principal works")]

The following are his principal works:

**Paintings**[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

* *Interior of a Farm at Dambaoh*, Salon, 1847
* *'Schlitteurs' of the Black Forest*, Salon, 1853
* *Potato Harvest during an Inundation*, Salon, 1852
* *Wood-Barge on the Rhine* ([engraved](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") by [Jazet](/wiki/Jean-Pierre-Marie_Jazet "Jean-Pierre-Marie Jazet")), [Paris Exhibition](/wiki/Exposition_Universelle_(1855) "Exposition Universelle (1855)"), 1855
* *Burial in the Vosges*, Paris Exhibition, 1855
* *La Fête-Dieu*, Paris Exhibition, 1855
* *The Miraculous Well*, Paris Exhibition, 1855
* *Mountebank in the Middle Ages*, Salon, 1857
* *Gathering Potatoes* (in the [Nantes Museum](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_des_Beaux-Arts_de_Nantes "Musée des Beaux-Arts de Nantes")), Salon, 1857
* *A Church Porch*, Salon, 1859
* *Burial on the Rhine*, Salon, 1859
* *The Skittle-Players*, Salon, 1859
* *A Protestant Marriage in Alsace* (etched by Rajon), Salon, 1861
* *The Wedding Feast* (etched by Bellin), Salon, 1861
* *The Blessing*, [London Exhibition](/wiki/1862_International_Exhibition "1862 International Exhibition"), 1862
* *The Pilgrims of St. Odile*, Salon, 1863
* *The End of the Deluge*, Salon, 1864
* *La Quète au Loup*, Salon, 1864
* *Reading the Bible in Alsace*, Salon, 1868
* *A Wedding in Alsace*, Salon, 1874 (earlier drawing of the same image pictured)
* *First Steps*, Salon 1876
* *The Réveil, Encampment of Pilgrims*, Salon, 1877

**Book illustrations**

* *Les Misérables*, by Victor Hugo (Paris: Pagnerre, 1862), first illustrated edition, with character portraits by Brion[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* *Notre-Dame de Paris* (*The Hunchback of Notre-Dame*), by Victor Hugo (Paris: J. Hetzel, 1864), illustrated by Brion; engravings by Yon and Perrichon[[9]](#cite_note-9)
